program.name: The Speed Machine 30-Day Precision Putting Training Program
program.author: Jarit Golf

speedZones.name: Touch
speedZones.description: Soft, touch putts for short distances

speedZones.name: Power
speedZones.description: Powerful putts for very long distances

speedZones.name: Maximum
speedZones.description: Maximum speed putts - use with caution

phases.name: Foundation
goals: Establish reliable motor programs for 3-10 MPH
Achieve 70%+ zone accuracy at Zone 1 speeds (3-7 MPH)
Achieve 50%+ zone accuracy at Zone 2 speeds (8-10 MPH)

phases.name: Development
phases.focus: Expand range into moderate speeds and introduce adaptive practice
goals: Command reliable motor programs for 3-16 MPH
Achieve 70%+ zone accuracy at Zone 1-2 speeds
Achieve 60%+ zone accuracy at Zone 3 speeds
Achieve 50%+ zone accuracy at Zone 4 introduction speeds

phases.name: Mastery
phases.focus: Full speed range with competition simulations and pressure protocols
goals: Achieve consistent performance under simulated pressure
Demonstrate competition-ready skills in Combine Mode

days.title: Speed Discovery
days.objective: Establish baseline speed awareness and familiarize yourself with the device feedback system. Today is about exploration, not perfection.

science.explanation: Motor learning research shows that the first exposure to a new skill should prioritize exploration over precision. Your brain needs to experience the range of possible outcomes before it can begin calibrating.
science.citation: Wulf, G., & Shea, C. H. (2002). Principles derived from the study of simple skills do not generalize to complex skill learning. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review.

blocks.name: Free Exploration
blocks.description: Hit putts at whatever speed feels natural. The app will learn from your results and begin tailoring future sessions to your needs.
focus: What is the range of your natural putting stroke?

blocks.name: Finding 4 MPH
blocks.description: Try to produce putts at exactly 4 MPH. This is a relatively soft putt — think of a short lag putt. After each stroke, note the actual speed, mentally adjust, and execute the next putt with the adjustment.

blocks.name: Finding 5 MPH
blocks.description: Repeat the process targeting 5 MPH. This requires slightly more force than 4 MPH. Notice how small the difference feels.

blocks.name: Alternating 4 and 5
blocks.description: Alternate between 4 and 5 MPH targets. This contrast training helps your brain distinguish between speeds. The app tracks your accuracy at each.

days.coachingNotes: Day 1 often feels frustrating. Your results may be inconsistent. This is completely normal — it's the brain's exploration phase gathering data. Trust the process.

days.title: Establishing 3, 4, and 5 MPH
days.objective: Build distinct motor programs for 3, 4, and 5 MPH through blocked practice. Today introduces 3 MPH — the softest speed in the program.

science.explanation: Blocked practice (repeating the same movement multiple times before switching) is optimal for skill acquisition in early learning stages. While it may feel less 'game-like,' it accelerates the formation of motor programs.
science.citation: Shea, J. B., & Morgan, R. L. (1979). Contextual interference effects on the acquisition, retention, and transfer of a motor skill. Journal of Experimental Psychology.

blocks.name: 3 MPH Development
blocks.description: 3 MPH is the softest putt in the program. Think of a 2-3 foot putt on a fast green.

blocks.name: Zone 1 Speed Development
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 1 practice — speeds selected from the touch zone based on your accuracy data. Your weakest speeds in the range appear more often. Focus on smooth, committed strokes.

blocks.name: Zone 1 Speed Challenge
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 1 challenge set — a second round weighted toward whichever Zone 1 speeds gave you the most trouble. Build consistency across the full touch zone range.

blocks.name: Sequential Challenge
blocks.description: Ladder sequence weighted toward the speeds you need the most work on. Reinforces distinctions between adjacent speeds.

days.coachingNotes: If you consistently hit 3.8 when targeting 3 MPH, consciously reduce your backswing length by approximately 20%. Specific mechanical adjustments beat vague effort adjustments.

days.title: Precision Refinement
days.objective: Refine accuracy at 3, 4, and 5 MPH. Today introduces self-assessment protocols that will be used throughout the program.

science.explanation: Self-assessment during motor learning enhances metacognition and accelerates skill development. Before each putt, you will predict your result. This prediction-feedback loop strengthens the connection between intention and execution.
science.citation: Guadagnoli, M. A., & Lee, T. D. (2004). Challenge point: A framework for conceptualizing the effects of various practice conditions in motor learning. Journal of Motor Behavior.

blocks.name: Warm-Up Review
blocks.description: Warm up across 3, 4, and 5 MPH to re-establish yesterday's patterns. Lightly weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: Zone 1 Predictive Practice
blocks.description: For each putt: (1) Set up, (2) Predict the speed before stroking, (3) Execute, (4) Compare prediction to actual. Speed is selected from Zone 1 based on your accuracy data.

blocks.name: Zone 1 Predictive Focus
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 1 predictive work targeting your weakest touch-zone speed. Small force variations have larger relative effects at low speeds — pay close attention to feel and prediction accuracy.

blocks.name: Zone 1 Predictive Build
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 1 predictive practice. Speeds weighted toward your weakest zone. Focus on accurately predicting the outcome before each stroke — the prediction habit is the skill being built here.

days.title: Consolidation and Gate Test Preparation
days.objective: Consolidate 3-5 MPH skills and prepare for tomorrow's Gate Test to unlock 6-7 MPH.

science.explanation: Consolidation occurs when skills transition from working memory to long-term procedural memory. This process happens during rest periods between practice sessions. Today's practice reinforces yesterday's learning while your brain continues to consolidate.
science.citation: Walker, M. P., et al. (2003). Practice with sleep makes perfect: Sleep-dependent motor skill learning. Neuron.

blocks.name: Rapid Alternation
blocks.description: Alternate between 3 and 5 MPH. This extreme contrast sharpens discrimination. The app adapts the mix based on your accuracy at each speed.

blocks.name: Fine Discrimination
blocks.description: Alternate between 4 and 5 MPH — much harder with only 1 MPH difference. The app adapts the mix based on your accuracy at each speed.

blocks.name: Gate Test Simulation
blocks.description: Practice the Gate Test format.

days.title: Gate Test and 6-7 MPH Introduction
days.objective: Pass the Zone 1 Gate Test and begin developing 6 and 7 MPH motor programs.

science.explanation: Gate Tests create 'desirable difficulties.' By requiring demonstrated competence before advancement, we ensure that new speeds are built on a solid foundation. Skipping gates typically results in regression and frustration.
science.citation: Bjork, R. A. (1994). Memory and metamemory considerations in the training of human beings. MIT Press.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Warm up across 3, 4, 5 MPH. Lightly weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: GATE TEST
blocks.onFail: Repeat Days 3-4 protocols. Attempt Gate Test again tomorrow.

blocks.name: 6 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: 6 MPH is 20% faster than 5 MPH. Should feel like a confident 10-foot putt. Focus on smooth tempo — don't jab at the ball.

blocks.name: 7 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: 7 MPH is the upper limit of Zone 1. This is approaching a 'firm' putt but still well within control range.

blocks.name: Full Zone 1 Integration
blocks.description: Adaptive practice across all Zone 1 speeds — weighted toward the speeds you need the most work on.

days.title: Zone 1 Full Range Development
days.objective: Develop fluency across the complete 3-7 MPH range with emphasis on the new 6-7 MPH speeds.

science.explanation: Variable practice within a constrained speed range (contextual interference) strengthens motor schema. By practicing multiple speeds within Zone 1, you develop flexible motor programs that can adapt to any speed within the zone.
science.citation: Schmidt, R. A. (1975). A schema theory of discrete motor skill learning. Psychological Review.

blocks.name: 6-7 MPH Blocked Practice
blocks.description: 12 putts at 6 MPH, then 12 putts at 7 MPH. These are still new speeds and benefit from blocked repetition.

blocks.name: Ladder Ascending
blocks.description: Up and down ladder pattern weighted toward your weaker speeds. Reinforces incremental relationships between speeds.

blocks.name: Random Zone 1
blocks.description: Adaptive practice across Zone 1 speeds — the app prioritizes the speeds you need the most work on.

blocks.name: Challenge Round
blocks.description: Consecutive putt challenge at your optimal Zone 1 speed — selected from 3–7 MPH based on where your accuracy is closest to the optimal challenge point. Record your best consecutive streak.

days.title: Pressure Introduction
days.objective: Introduce mild pressure conditions while maintaining Zone 1 accuracy. Pressure-proofing begins early.

science.explanation: Skills learned under some pressure transfer better to high-pressure situations. Today's 'consequence' drills begin building pressure tolerance.
science.citation: Baumeister, R. F. (1984). Choking under pressure: Self-consciousness and paradoxical effects of incentives on skillful performance. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Warm up across Zone 1 speeds. Lightly weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: Make 5 in a Row Challenge
blocks.description: Hit 5 consecutive putts within zone at your optimal challenge speed — selected from Zone 1 based on your current accuracy data. If you miss, restart at zero.

blocks.name: Elimination Ladder
blocks.description: Start at 3 MPH. If in zone, move to 4 MPH. If not, restart at 3. Goal: Climb from 3 to 7 without missing. Record highest reached.

blocks.name: Recovery Practice
blocks.description: Confidence-building recovery. Speed selected from Zone 1 based on your strongest results — end the session feeling solid and in control.

days.title: Zone 2 Gate Test Preparation
days.objective: Achieve peak Zone 1 performance in preparation for tomorrow's Gate Test to unlock Zone 2 speeds (8-10 MPH).

science.explanation: 'Overlearning' — continued practice after achieving proficiency — increases retention and automaticity. Today's high-volume Zone 1 practice solidifies these speeds before introducing the new challenge of Zone 2.
science.citation: Driskell, J. E., Willis, R. P., & Copper, C. (1992). Effect of overlearning on retention. Journal of Applied Psychology.

blocks.name: Zone 1 Mastery Drill
blocks.description: Adaptive sequence weighted toward your weaker speeds. Goal: 75% zone accuracy across all speeds.

blocks.name: Random Zone 1 Assessment
blocks.description: Adaptive practice weighted toward your weaker speeds. Check your Stats to track progress heading into the Gate Test.

blocks.name: Gate Test Simulation
blocks.description: To pass the real test tomorrow: Target 80%+ (10+ of 12), Zone 65%+ (8+ of 12).

days.title: Zone 2 Gate Test and Introduction
days.objective: Pass the Zone 2 Gate Test and begin developing 8, 9, and 10 MPH motor programs. This is a significant speed increase — approach with focus.

science.explanation: Zone 2 speeds represent a transition from 'touch' to 'firm' putting. Research on force production shows that larger forces have inherently more variability, which is why our tolerance expands from ±0.5 to ±0.7 MPH.
science.citation: Carlton, L. G. (1994). The effects of temporal-loss of feedback on motor performance. Journal of Motor Behavior.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Warm up across 5, 6, 7 MPH. Lightly weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: ZONE 2 GATE TEST
blocks.onFail: Return to Day 8 protocol. Attempt Gate Test again tomorrow.

blocks.name: 8 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: 8 MPH is noticeably firmer than 7 MPH. Represents approximately a 20-25 foot putt on medium-speed greens.

blocks.name: 9 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: Focus on maintaining a smooth stroke despite the increased force.

blocks.name: 10 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: 10 MPH is the top of Zone 2 and represents a firm 30+ foot putt. Requires commitment to the stroke.

blocks.name: Cool-Down
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 1 cool-down. Speed selected from your strongest touch-zone results — re-center your speed sense on a confident, relaxed note.

warnings.message: Zone 2 speeds hit the device harder. Ensure proper setup and focus on target accuracy. If you miss the device opening, the ball will strike the device body.

days.title: Phase 1 Assessment
days.objective: Complete Phase 1 with a comprehensive assessment of 3-10 MPH capabilities. Establish benchmark scores for comparison after Phase 2 and 3.

science.explanation: Periodic assessment serves multiple purposes: it provides objective feedback on progress, identifies weaknesses requiring attention, and creates 'testing effect' benefits that strengthen memory.
science.citation: Roediger, H. L., & Karpicke, J. D. (2006). Test-enhanced learning: Taking memory tests improves long-term retention. Psychological Science.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Warm up across the full 3-10 MPH range. Lightly weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: PHASE 1 ASSESSMENT
blocks.description: 6 putts at each speed in order. Record target and zone accuracy at each speed.

blocks.name: Best Speed Challenge
blocks.description: Choose your strongest speed from 3-10 MPH — check your Stats to see which one. Hit 16 consecutive putts and go for a personal best.

days.coachingNotes: If you meet these standards, proceed to Phase 2 tomorrow. If not, repeat Days 8-10 until standards are met. Check your Stats to see exactly which speeds need the most work — the app will continue to adapt your practice accordingly.

days.title: Zone 2 Consolidation
days.objective: Strengthen Zone 2 speed control before expanding into Zone 3. The app automatically focuses on the speeds you need the most work on.

science.explanation: The transition between phases benefits from a consolidation day. This allows overnight memory consolidation to strengthen new skills before adding complexity.
science.citation: Walker, M. P., et al. (2002). Practice with sleep makes perfect: Sleep-dependent motor skill learning. Neuron.

blocks.name: Zone 1 Maintenance
blocks.description: Adaptive sequence across 3-7 MPH to maintain foundational skills — weighted toward speeds that need the most work.

blocks.name: Zone 2 Blocked Practice
blocks.description: Blocked practice at Zone 2 speeds. Check your Stats to see your per-speed accuracy across 8, 9, and 10 MPH.

blocks.name: Transition Practice
blocks.description: Adaptive cross-zone practice between Zone 1 and Zone 2 — weighted toward your weaker speeds. Large jumps challenge your calibration.

blocks.name: Zone 2 Assessment
blocks.description: Goal: 75%+ zone accuracy to confirm readiness for Zone 3.

days.title: Zone 3 Gate Test and 11 MPH Introduction
days.objective: Pass the Zone 3 Gate Test and introduce 11 MPH — your first 'firm' speed with the expanded ±1.0 MPH tolerance.

science.explanation: Zone 3 represents a qualitative shift in putting force. The tolerance expansion to ±1.0 MPH reflects the increased variability inherent in producing larger forces.
science.citation: Schmidt, R. A., et al. (1979). Motor-output variability: A theory for the accuracy of rapid motor acts. Psychological Review.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Warm up across 7, 8, 9 MPH. Lightly weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: 11 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: 11 MPH is your entry into Zone 3. This is a firm, committed stroke. New tolerance: ±1.0 MPH.

blocks.name: 8-11 Integration
blocks.description: Adaptive transition practice between Zone 2 and the new 11 MPH — weighted toward speeds that need the most work.

blocks.name: Cool-Down
blocks.description: Adaptive cool-down across Zones 1–2. Speed selected from your strongest recent results — close out the gate test day on a confidence-building note.

warnings.message: Zone 3 speeds deliver more force. If target accuracy drops below 70%, stop and return to Zone 2 practice.

days.title: 12-13 MPH Development
days.objective: Develop motor programs for 12 and 13 MPH while maintaining Zone 1-2 skills.

science.explanation: Adding speeds incrementally allows the motor system to use existing programs as references. Your 11 MPH program becomes the foundation for 12 and 13 MPH.
science.citation: Schmidt, R. A. (1975). A schema theory of discrete motor skill learning. Psychological Review.

blocks.name: Zone 1-2 Quick Review
blocks.description: Adaptive maintenance sequence — weighted toward your weaker foundational speeds.

blocks.name: Zone 3 Reinforcement
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 3 reinforcement — speeds from 9–14 MPH selected based on your current accuracy data. Solidify your mid-range feel with reps where you need them most.

blocks.name: 12 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: 12 MPH is only 9% faster than 11 MPH but requires noticeable adjustment.

blocks.name: 13 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: 13 MPH is approximately a 40-foot putt on medium greens.

blocks.name: Zone 3 Integration
blocks.description: Adaptive transitions within Zone 3 — weighted toward the speeds you need the most work on.

days.title: 14 MPH and Zone 3 Integration
days.objective: Complete Zone 3 with the introduction of 14 MPH and integrate all Zone 3 speeds.

science.explanation: 14 MPH represents the upper boundary of Zone 3 and the last speed before the tolerance expands again. Mastering this ceiling speed creates a stable platform for Zone 4.
science.citation: Guadagnoli, M. A., & Lee, T. D. (2004). Challenge point framework. Journal of Motor Behavior.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Adaptive warm-up progression through learned speeds — weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: 14 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: 14 MPH is the top of Zone 3. This is a powerful putt. Focus on committed stroke with smooth tempo.

blocks.name: Full Zone 3 Practice
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 3 practice — weighted toward the Zone 3 speeds you need the most work on.

blocks.name: Cross-Zone Challenge
blocks.description: Adaptive cross-zone jumps weighted toward your weaker speeds. Extreme contextual interference builds robust calibration.

days.title: Random Practice Introduction
days.objective: Shift from blocked to adaptive practice across all unlocked speeds. The app now intelligently weights your weaker speeds more heavily.

science.explanation: The contextual interference effect demonstrates that varied practice, while harder during acquisition, produces superior retention and transfer. The app now strategically weights this variation toward your weakest speeds for maximum improvement.
science.citation: Shea, J. B., & Morgan, R. L. (1979). Contextual interference effects. Journal of Experimental Psychology.

blocks.name: Full Range Random
blocks.description: Adaptive full-range sequence across 3-14 MPH — weighted toward the speeds you need the most work on.

blocks.name: Extreme Jumps
blocks.description: Maximum contrast practice — adaptive sequence weighted toward your weaker speeds at each end of the range.

blocks.name: Call Your Shot
blocks.description: The app serves you speeds weighted toward your weaknesses. React and execute each one — no planning ahead.

days.title: Full Range Fluency
days.objective: Develop seamless transitions between any two speeds in your current range.

science.explanation: Fluency represents the ability to access any motor program quickly and accurately. This requires robust memory traces that can be retrieved under various conditions.
science.citation: Schmidt, R. A., & Lee, T. D. (2011). Motor control and learning: A behavioral emphasis. Human Kinetics.

blocks.name: Zone Jumping
blocks.description: Adaptive zone-jumping sequence weighted toward your weaker speeds across all three zones.

blocks.name: Consecutive Challenge
blocks.description: Start at 3 MPH. Each successful in-zone putt advances +1 MPH. Each miss returns to 3. Goal: Reach 14 in one unbroken sequence.

blocks.name: Reverse Ladder
blocks.description: Descending ladder weighted toward your weaker speeds. Reducing force precisely is often harder than adding it.

days.title: Advanced Pressure Protocols
days.objective: Increase pressure tolerance with more demanding challenges.

science.explanation: Progressive pressure exposure builds 'stress inoculation.' By practicing under incrementally increasing pressure, you develop coping mechanisms that prevent choking.
science.citation: Beilock, S. L., & Carr, T. H. (2001). On the fragility of skilled performance. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Warm up across the full range. Lightly weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: 7 in a Row Challenge
blocks.description: Hit 7 consecutive putts within zone at your optimal challenge speed — selected from 7–11 MPH based on your accuracy data. Significantly harder than 5 in a row.

blocks.name: Elimination Tournament
blocks.description: You have 3 'lives.' Random speeds. Each miss costs one life. Count in-zone putts before losing all lives.

blocks.name: Recovery
blocks.description: Adaptive recovery practice — speed selected from 6–10 MPH based on your strongest recent results. Return to confident, controlled putting before tomorrow.

days.title: Zone 4 Gate Test Preparation
days.objective: Achieve peak Zone 3 performance before tomorrow's Gate Test for Zone 4 (15-18 MPH).

science.explanation: The transition to Zone 4 represents a significant increase in force production. Thorough preparation reduces the risk of regression when new speeds are introduced.
science.citation: Guadagnoli, M. A., & Lee, T. D. (2004). Challenge point framework. Journal of Motor Behavior.

blocks.name: Zone 3 Mastery Drill
blocks.description: Goal: 65% zone accuracy.

blocks.name: Gate Test Simulation
blocks.description: To pass tomorrow: Target 85%+ (10+ of 12), Zone 70%+ (8+ of 12).

blocks.name: Mental Preparation
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 3–4 preparation — speeds from 11–16 MPH selected based on where you need the most work. Focus on committed, confident strokes ahead of tomorrow's gate test.

days.title: Zone 4 Gate Test and 15-16 MPH Introduction
days.objective: Pass the Zone 4 Gate Test and introduce 15 and 16 MPH with their new ±1.25 MPH tolerance.

science.explanation: Zone 4 speeds (15-18 MPH) represent powerful putts rarely needed on course but essential for complete speed control. The expanded tolerance reflects the physics of high-force production.
science.citation: Schmidt, R. A., et al. (1979). Motor-output variability theory. Psychological Review.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Warm up across 10, 12, 14 MPH. Lightly weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: 15 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: 15 MPH enters Zone 4. New tolerance: ±1.25 MPH. This is a powerful stroke. Focus on staying smooth despite the force increase.

blocks.name: 16 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: 16 MPH continues Zone 4 development.

blocks.name: Cool-Down
blocks.description: Adaptive mid-range cool-down. Speed selected from 7–11 MPH based on your strongest recent results — close out the gate test day on a solid note.

warnings.message: Zone 4 speeds deliver significant force. If target accuracy drops below 65%, STOP immediately and return to Zone 3.

days.title: Phase 2 Assessment
days.objective: Complete Phase 2 with comprehensive assessment across 3-16 MPH.

science.explanation: This assessment serves as both evaluation and retrieval practice. The testing effect will strengthen all speed programs being assessed.
science.citation: Roediger, H. L., & Karpicke, J. D. (2006). Test-enhanced learning. Psychological Science.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Warm up across the full range. Lightly weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: PHASE 2 ASSESSMENT
blocks.description: 4 putts at each speed. Record target and zone accuracy.

blocks.name: Best Speed Challenge
blocks.description: Choose your strongest speed from 3-16 MPH — check your Stats to see which one. Hit 16 consecutive putts and go for a personal best.

days.coachingNotes: If you meet standards, proceed to Phase 3. If not, repeat Days 18-20 until standards are met. Check your Stats to see exactly which speeds need the most work — the app will continue to adapt your practice accordingly.

days.title: 17 MPH Introduction
days.objective: Introduce 17 MPH while maintaining accuracy at all previously learned speeds.

science.explanation: 17 MPH represents continued Zone 4 development. Your established 16 MPH program provides the foundation for this incremental increase.
science.citation: Schmidt, R. A. (1975). Schema theory. Psychological Review.

blocks.name: Zone 1-3 Maintenance
blocks.description: Adaptive review of foundational speeds — weighted toward the ones you need the most work on.

blocks.name: 15-16 MPH Reinforcement
blocks.description: Blocked practice at Zone 4 speeds.

blocks.name: 17 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: 17 MPH continues Zone 4. Focus on committed stroke with controlled finish.

blocks.name: 15-17 Integration
blocks.description: Adaptive transitions within Zone 4 — weighted toward the speeds you need the most work on.

days.title: 18 MPH Development
days.objective: Complete Zone 4 with 18 MPH — the last speed before the maximum-force Zone 5.

science.explanation: 18 MPH represents the upper boundary of Zone 4 and requires significant force production. Mastery here prepares you for the Zone 5 challenge.
science.citation: Guadagnoli, M. A., & Lee, T. D. (2004). Challenge point framework. Journal of Motor Behavior.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Adaptive progressive warm-up — weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: Zone 4 Reinforcement
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 4 reinforcement — speeds from 15–18 MPH selected based on your current accuracy data. Consolidate your high-speed feel with reps at the right challenge level.

blocks.name: 18 MPH Introduction
blocks.description: 18 MPH is the top of Zone 4. This is a powerful stroke requiring full commitment.

blocks.name: Zone 4 Full Integration
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 4 practice — weighted toward the Zone 4 speeds you need the most work on.

warnings.message: 18 MPH putts deliver substantial force. Maintain focus on target accuracy above all else.

days.title: Zone 4 Mastery
days.objective: Achieve full Zone 4 fluency across 15-18 MPH.

science.explanation: Before adding Zone 5 speeds, Zone 4 must be thoroughly consolidated. This overlearning creates stable motor programs that resist interference from new learning.
science.citation: Driskell, J. E., et al. (1992). Effect of overlearning on retention. Journal of Applied Psychology.

blocks.name: Full Range Random
blocks.description: Adaptive full-range practice across 3-18 MPH — weighted toward the speeds you need the most work on.

blocks.name: Zone 4 Intensive
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 4 intensive — extra focus on your weaker Zone 4 speeds.

blocks.name: Pressure Test
blocks.description: High-speed consecutive challenge — speed selected from 13–17 MPH based on your optimal challenge point. Pressure calibrated to your current ability level.

days.title: Zone 5 Gate Test Preparation
days.objective: Prepare for the Zone 5 Gate Test — the final gate before maximum speeds.

science.explanation: Zone 5 (19-20 MPH) represents the practical maximum for putting speed. The gate test ensures you have the control necessary to practice safely at these forces.
science.citation: Guadagnoli, M. A., & Lee, T. D. (2004). Challenge point framework. Journal of Motor Behavior.

blocks.name: Zone 4 Peak Performance
blocks.description: Goal: 60% zone accuracy.

blocks.name: Gate Test Simulation
blocks.description: To pass tomorrow: Target 85%+ (10+ of 12), Zone 60%+ (7+ of 12).

blocks.name: Mental Preparation
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 5 preparation — speeds from 16–20 MPH selected based on where you need the most work. Visualize success at maximum speeds. Commit to safety protocols.

days.title: Zone 5 Gate Test and 19 MPH Introduction
days.objective: Pass the Zone 5 Gate Test and carefully introduce 19 MPH with maximum caution.

science.explanation: Zone 5 speeds use the ±1.5 MPH tolerance, the widest in the program. This reflects the substantial variability in maximum-force production.
science.citation: Schmidt, R. A., et al. (1979). Motor-output variability theory. Psychological Review.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Warm up across 14, 16, 18 MPH. Lightly weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: 19 MPH CAREFUL Introduction
blocks.description: 19 MPH enters Zone 5. New tolerance: ±1.5 MPH. Start with 5 putts at 18.5 MPH to bridge the gap. Then attempt 19 MPH. STOP if 3 consecutive misses.

blocks.name: 17-19 Integration
blocks.description: Gradual transitions only.

blocks.name: Cool-Down
blocks.description: Adaptive cool-down at upper mid-range speeds. Selected from 8–12 MPH based on your strongest recent results.

warnings.message: MAXIMUM CAUTION: 19 MPH delivers extreme force. Emergency Protocol: Any 3 consecutive misses = reduce to 16 MPH immediately.

days.title: 20 MPH Introduction
days.objective: Introduce the program maximum speed of 20 MPH with extreme caution.

science.explanation: 20 MPH represents the practical ceiling for putting speed. Few on-course situations require this force, but having the capability ensures complete speed control.
science.citation: Schmidt, R. A., & Lee, T. D. (2011). Motor control and learning. Human Kinetics.

blocks.name: Full Range Review
blocks.description: Adaptive review across all speeds — weighted toward the ones you need the most work on.

blocks.name: Zone 5 Reinforcement
blocks.description: Adaptive Zone 5 reinforcement — speeds from 17–20 MPH selected based on your current accuracy data. Solidify your maximum speed feel in the final push week.

blocks.name: 20 MPH EXTREME CAUTION Introduction
blocks.description: 20 MPH is the program maximum. Start with 5 putts at 19.5 MPH. Then attempt 20 MPH. STOP IMMEDIATELY if control deteriorates.
safetyChecklist: Entry requirement: 70% target at 19 MPH achieved
Maximum attempts: 15 putts at 20 MPH today

emergencyProtocol.trigger: 3 consecutive misses OR target accuracy below 60%
emergencyProtocol.action: Stop immediately. Do not attempt again today.

blocks.name: 18-20 Integration
blocks.description: Careful transitions only.

blocks.name: Cool-Down
blocks.description: Adaptive cool-down at mid-range speeds. Selected from 8–12 MPH based on your strongest results — finish the final push week on a confident, controlled note.

warnings.message: EXTREME CAUTION PROTOCOL: 20 MPH is the maximum program speed. If target accuracy falls below 60%, STOP and do not attempt again today.

days.title: Full Range Random Practice
days.objective: Develop fluency across the complete 3-20 MPH range with adaptive practice weighted toward your weaknesses.

science.explanation: Having unlocked all speeds, the focus shifts to building robust, flexible motor control. Adaptive practice across the full range — weighted toward your weaknesses — creates the most transferable skill.
science.citation: Shea, J. B., & Morgan, R. L. (1979). Contextual interference effects. Journal of Experimental Psychology.

blocks.name: Complete Range Random
blocks.description: Adaptive practice across all 18 speeds — weighted toward the ones you need the most work on.

blocks.name: Extreme Contrast
blocks.description: Maximum contrast jumps weighted toward your weaker speeds. Tests your calibration limits.

blocks.name: Zone Sweeps
blocks.description: Adaptive zone sweep pattern weighted toward your weaker speeds across all five zones.

days.title: Competition Preparation
days.objective: Prepare for tomorrow's competition simulation with advanced pressure protocols.

science.explanation: Competition simulation combines all learned skills under pressure conditions that approximate actual performance demands.
science.citation: Beilock, S. L., & Carr, T. H. (2001). On the fragility of skilled performance. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Warm up at self-selected speeds, building confidence. Lightly weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: Perfect 10 Challenge
blocks.description: Hit 10 consecutive putts at any speed that are both on-target AND in-zone. You may change speeds, but each must succeed.

blocks.name: Combine Mode Preview
blocks.description: Switch device to Combine Mode. Complete one full session as practice for tomorrow. Record your score.

days.title: Competition Simulation
days.objective: Demonstrate competition-ready performance in multiple Combine Mode sessions.

science.explanation: Competition represents the ultimate test of skill transfer. Multiple sessions under pressure conditions validate your 29 days of training.
science.citation: Masters, R. S. W. (1992). Knowledge, knerves and know-how. British Journal of Psychology.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Adaptive progressive warm-up — weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: Competition Session 1
blocks.description: Combine Mode. Complete full session. Record score.

blocks.name: Brief Reset
blocks.description: Free practice at comfortable speed.

blocks.name: Competition Session 2
blocks.description: Combine Mode. Compare to Session 1.

blocks.name: Competition Session 3
blocks.description: If time permits, begin third session. Tests sustained performance.

days.title: Final Assessment
days.objective: Complete the program with a comprehensive final assessment across all 18 speeds.

science.explanation: This final assessment measures the cumulative effect of 30 days of deliberate practice. The testing effect from this assessment will further strengthen all motor programs.
science.citation: Roediger, H. L., & Karpicke, J. D. (2006). Test-enhanced learning. Psychological Science.

blocks.name: Warm-Up
blocks.description: Final warm-up to peak readiness. Lightly weighted toward speeds that need attention.

blocks.name: FINAL ASSESSMENT
blocks.description: 4 putts at each speed from 3-20 MPH (72 total). Record target and zone accuracy at each speed.

blocks.name: Victory Lap
blocks.description: Choose your three favorite speeds. Hit 5 putts at each, plus 1 final putt at 10 MPH — the middle of your range.

days.coachingNotes: CONGRATULATIONS! You have completed the 30-Day Precision Putting Training Program. You now command speed control across the full 3-20 MPH range. Check your Stats to see how far you've come — your speed ladder tells the full story of your progress.

scientificFoundation.explanation: Skills are learned best when practice conditions closely match performance conditions. The Speed Machine provides practice at exact speeds that transfer directly to on-course putting.
scientificFoundation.application: Every putt targets a specific MPH corresponding to real putting distances.

citation.authors: Schmidt, R. A., & Lee, T. D.
citation.title: Motor control and learning: A behavioral emphasis

scientificFoundation.explanation: Blocked practice builds initial competence quickly. Random practice produces better retention and transfer.
scientificFoundation.application: Phase 1 uses blocked practice; Phases 2-3 introduce adaptive practice weighted toward the user's weakest speeds.

citation.authors: Shea, J. B., & Morgan, R. L.
citation.title: Contextual interference effects on the acquisition, retention, and transfer of a motor skill
citation.journal: Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Learning and Memory

scientificFoundation.explanation: High contextual interference during practice leads to superior long-term retention and transfer.
scientificFoundation.application: Zone jumping drills create high interference by requiring rapid switches between different speeds, with adaptive weighting to emphasize the user's weakest areas.

citation.authors: Magill, R. A., & Hall, K. G.
citation.title: A review of the contextual interference effect in motor skill acquisition

scientificFoundation.explanation: Immediate, precise feedback accelerates motor learning by allowing comparison of intended and actual outcomes.
scientificFoundation.application: The Speed Machine provides instant MPH feedback after every putt.

citation.authors: Salmoni, A. W., Schmidt, R. A., & Walter, C. B.
citation.title: Knowledge of results and motor learning: A review and critical reappraisal

scientificFoundation.explanation: Skills develop optimally when challenge increases incrementally, matched to learner skill level.
scientificFoundation.application: Gate Test system ensures new speeds are introduced only after demonstrated competence.

citation.authors: Guadagnoli, M. A., & Lee, T. D.
citation.title: Challenge point: A framework for conceptualizing the effects of various practice conditions in motor learning

scientificFoundation.explanation: Skills learned under some pressure transfer better to high-pressure situations. Explicit monitoring can cause 'choking.'
scientificFoundation.application: Pressure drills introduced early. Focus on speed numbers reduces explicit monitoring.

citation.title: Choking under pressure: Self-consciousness and paradoxical effects of incentives on skillful performance
citation.journal: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology

scientificFoundation.explanation: Motor skills are consolidated during rest periods, particularly sleep. Spaced practice enhances retention.
scientificFoundation.application: 30-day structure with daily sessions allows consolidation between practices.

citation.authors: Walker, M. P., Brakefield, T., Morgan, A., Hobson, J. A., & Stickgold, R.
citation.title: Practice with sleep makes perfect: Sleep-dependent motor skill learning

scientificFoundation.explanation: Retrieval practice (testing) strengthens memory more than additional study.
scientificFoundation.application: Regular Gate Tests and assessments serve as retrieval practice.

citation.authors: Roediger, H. L., & Karpicke, J. D.
citation.title: Test-enhanced learning: Taking memory tests improves long-term retention

scientificFoundation.explanation: Motor variability increases with force magnitude, following Weber's Law.
scientificFoundation.application: Tolerance zones expand from ±0.5 MPH to ±1.5 MPH at higher speeds.

citation.authors: Schmidt, R. A., Zelaznik, H., Hawkins, B., Frank, J. S., & Quinn Jr, J. T.
citation.title: Motor-output variability: A theory for the accuracy of rapid motor acts

scientificFoundation.explanation: With sufficient practice, skills become automatic and more robust under pressure.
scientificFoundation.application: High volume (~2,000+ putts) drives skills toward automaticity.

citation.authors: Fitts, P. M., & Posner, M. I.
citation.title: Human performance

scientificFoundation.explanation: Continued practice after achieving proficiency increases retention and resistance to forgetting.
scientificFoundation.application: Program continues practicing established speeds even after mastery.

citation.authors: Driskell, J. E., Willis, R. P., & Copper, C.
citation.title: Effect of overlearning on retention

scientificFoundation.explanation: Skills learned implicitly are more robust under pressure than explicitly learned skills.
scientificFoundation.application: Focus on single number (MPH) rather than mechanical checkpoints promotes implicit learning.

citation.title: Knowledge, knerves and know-how: The role of explicit versus implicit knowledge in the breakdown of a complex motor skill under pressure

graphics.title: Speed Zone Reference Card
graphics.description: Wallet-sized card with all five zones, tolerances, and distance equivalents

graphics.title: Progress Tracking Sheet
graphics.description: Printable daily sheet for recording date, zone accuracy, and notes

graphics.title: Gate Test Checklist
graphics.description: Printable checklist for each Gate Test with required putts, pass criteria, and results space

graphics.title: Speed-to-Distance Conversion Chart
graphics.description: Comprehensive chart showing MPH 3-20 with distances at stimp 9, 10, 11, 12, 13

graphics.title: Program Overview Infographic
graphics.description: Visual summary of 30-day timeline, three phases, speed unlocking, and milestones
